title: Math — Systems of Linear Equations
description: SAT-style systems of linear equations questions: substitution, elimination, infinite/no solutions, and parameter conditions.

questions.prompt:
If the system of equations below has infinitely many solutions, what is the value of \(k\)?

\(kx + 6y = 12\)

\(2x + 3y = 6\)
questions.explanation: For infinitely many solutions, the equations must be scalar multiples. Doubling \(2x+3y=6\) gives \(4x+6y=12\), so \(k=4\).

questions.prompt:
What is the solution to the system of equations?

\(y = 2x - 5\)

\(3x - y = 11\)

Which ordered pair satisfies the system of equations?

\(2x + y = 7\)

\(x - y = 1\)
questions.explanation: Add the equations: \((2x+y)+(x-y)=7+1\Rightarrow 3x=8\Rightarrow x=\frac{8}{3}\). Then \(x-y=1\Rightarrow y=\frac{8}{3}-1=\frac{5}{3}\).

questions.prompt:
If the system below has no solution, which statement must be true?

\(y = 4x - 2\)

\(y = 4x + 5\)
choices: The lines intersect at one point
questions.explanation: Both lines have slope 4 but different y-intercepts, so they are parallel and never intersect (no solution).

questions.prompt:
For what value of \(a\) does the system have exactly one solution?

\(ax + 2y = 4\)

\(4x + 8y = 10\)
questions.explanation: Exactly one solution happens when the lines have different slopes. First line: \(y=-\frac{a}{2}x+2\). Second line: \(y=-\frac{1}{2}x+\frac{10}{8}\). Slopes differ when \(-\frac{a}{2}\ne -\frac{1}{2}\Rightarrow a\ne 1\).

questions.prompt: Which system has infinitely many solutions?
questions.explanation: The second equation \(4x+6y=12\) is exactly 2 times the first \(2x+3y=6\), so they represent the same line (infinitely many solutions).
